medications.drug_name: high-potency topical corticosteroid (re-issue usual, e.g. betamethasone dipropionate)
medications.indication_rationale: Restart the patient's usual high-potency topical steroid that was stopped; likely to settle the flare. Apply to plaques with generous emollient over the top.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to affected plaques as previously prescribed

medications.indication_rationale: Generous emollients reduce fissuring, protect the barrier and ease pain.

medications.drug_name: IM ketorolac / injectable NSAID over chat
medications.indication_rationale: Injections cannot be given via telehealth; simple oral analgesia within limits suffices for the skin pain while arranging review.

medications.drug_name: unproven agents (Wahls diet, high-dose vitamin D)
medications.indication_rationale: No evidence base for controlling an acute psoriasis flare.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Dermatology referral for systemic/biologic therapy given extensive disease.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: Cool environment, loose cotton clothing, gentle skin care
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Reduce heat/friction and infection risk over fissures.
Limited evidence; low harm, patient-preference option, not a flare treatment.
No evidence for controlling a psoriasis flare.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch for sudden pus-filled bumps, a high fever/shivering, a spreading hot red area, honey-coloured crusts or clustered blisters, or feeling very unwell, and go to ED if any occur.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Safety-nets pustular psoriasis, erythroderma and secondary infection.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Restart the topical steroid with thick emollient over it, keep the room cool, wear loose cotton, and keep hydrated.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Settles the flare and protects the skin barrier.

patient_education_points.point: Your psoriasis has flared badly, partly from stress and running out of your ointment — restarting the steroid ointment with plenty of moisturiser should start to settle the cracking over a day or two.
Because it's so widespread and cracked, you also need a prompt in-person/skin-specialist review — extensive psoriasis like this often needs stronger (systemic) treatment.
Keep the room cool, wear loose cotton, moisturise heavily, and take care of the cracked skin to reduce infection risk; simple painkillers within limits can help.
Go to ED if your skin suddenly fills with small pus-filled bumps, you get a high fever with shivering, one area becomes rapidly hotter/redder and spreading, or you feel very unwell.

follow_up_plan.interval: Prompt in-person GP/dermatology review within a few days for systemic-therapy planning; sooner if worsening
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Skin extent/secondary infection, systemic features, topical/systemic therapy, hydration/skin care
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Pustular eruption, high fever/rigors, spreading infection or systemic instability — go to ED.

must_include_items: Screen and safety-net pustular psoriasis, erythroderma instability and secondary sepsis/cellulitis/eczema herpeticum
Restart the usual high-potency topical steroid plus generous emollients and cooling/skin care
Arrange prompt in-person/dermatology review for systemic-therapy planning
Avoid remote injections and unproven diets/supplements as therapy
acceptable_alternatives: Omega-3/vitamin D mentioned neutrally as low-evidence
errors_of_commission: Presenting Wahls/high-dose vitamin D as therapy
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires (1) pustular/erythroderma/infection safety-netting, (2) restarting topical steroid + emollients, and (3) a prompt in-person/dermatology review pathway. Just re-sending the ointment with no safety-netting is a fail.
